data.title: BHP, Faraday Copper explore deal to restart Arizona copper mine
data.description:
BHP (BHP) said Friday it signed a non-binding letter of intent with Faraday Copper (CPPKF) to evaluate restarting the San Manuel copper mine in Arizona and combining it with Faraday's nearby Copper Creek project to create a larger copper development hub.

Under the proposed terms, Faraday (CPPKF) would acquire BHP's (BHP) San Manuel property in exchange for a 30% interest in the equity of Faraday, as well as commensurate shareholder and marketing rights; BHP also agreed to participate in any Faraday equity raises for two years, investing up to $20M.

Operating during 1955-99, the San Manuel mine was one of the most significant copper producers in the U.S. and the largest domestic underground copper mine at the time of closure; the mine was closed in 1999 following a period of sustained low copper prices.

BHP (BHP) said it looks forward to working with Faraday (CPPKF) to "create a pathway to bring on additional U.S. copper supply to the market, [which] would support the U.S. objective of greater copper supply chain resilience."

data.title: Apparel, auto stocks rally as SCOTUS strikes down Trump tariffs
data.description:
The U.S. Supreme Court ruled in a 6-3 decision to strike down President Trump’s tariffs, sending shares of companies heavily dependent on foreign manufacturing higher on Friday.

The ruling found that President Trump did not have the authority to impose tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. Under the act, President Trump sought to address two foreign threats, including the influx of illegal drugs from Canada, Mexico, and China, and the “large and persistent” trade deficits.

The President determined that the drug influx had “created a public health crisis” and that the trade deficits had “led to the hollowing out” of the American manufacturing base and “undermined critical supply chains.”

While the court ruled that the president did not have the power to impose tariffs, it did address eligibility for tariff refunds.

Of the nine members of SCOTUS, dissenting opinions were written by Justices Kavanaugh, Thomas, and Alito.

The decision is having a significant impact on shares of companies that remain vulnerable to overseas manufacturing despite recent mitigation actions, including Nike (NKE), On Holdings (ONON), Victoria’s Secret (VSCO), RH (RH), Deckers Outdoors (DECK), lululemon athletica (LULU), Williams Sonoma (WSM), and automakers General Motors (GM), and Stellantis (STLA), all of which had an outsized reaction to the decision.

data.title: Wall Street moves higher as market participants assess GDP and PCE data
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages move higher on Friday as investors react to the PCE report, which came in hotter than expected.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.6%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.4%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.3%. 

From a sector vantage point, seven of the S&P 500’s 11 segments were in positive trading territory, with communication services at the top. Going the other way, energy has struggled the most so far. 

Treasury yields inched higher as the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) moved up by 2 basis points to 3.48%, and the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved up by 2 basis points to 4.09%. 

On the economic calendar, PCE inflation ticked up in December and came in higher than the consensus. The Q4 U.S. GDP (first estimate) came in at +1.4%, annual rate, vs. +2.8% consensus and +4.4% in the prior quarter.

“Friday's GDP for the fourth quarter was much lower-than-expected, but it was still positive and shows that the economy expanded at respectable clip during the final few months of 2025 even as fears about the labor market started to intensify. We expect a strong year of economic growth in 2026, driven by business investment, consumer spending and fading trade headwinds,” Rick Gardner, chief investment officer of RGA Investments noted.

As for stocks that were on the move, shares of Corning Incorporated (GLW) climbed 6.1%, and shares of Intel (INTC) came down by 2.8%.

data.title: Top 10 Oil And Gas Equipment Services Stocks by Year-To-Date Performance
data.description:
Year to date, the energy sector has been one of the market’s strongest performers. In light of this, below is a list of the top 10 oil and gas equipment and services stocks ranked by their YTD performance as of February 2026.

The list features companies with market capitalizations ranging from approximately $600 million to over $60 billion, all operating primarily in the United States.

The list is topped by National Energy Services Reunited (NESR), with a YTD performance exceeding 54%. Oceaneering International (OII) and Oil States International (OIS) follow as the next highest performers, both securing gains above 47%. Tidewater (TDW), Liberty Energy (LBRT), and Helix Energy Solutions Group (HLX) round out the middle of the pack with returns between 45% and 46%.

The group features diversity in Quant ratings, with “Strong Buy” ratings for National Energy Services Reunited (NESR) at 4.98 and Forum Energy Technologies (FET) at 4.86. Industry giants such as Baker Hughes Company (BKR) and TechnipFMC plc (FTI) also appear among the top year-to-date movers, though they carry “Hold” ratings.

Here is the list:

  1. National Energy Services Reunited (NESR), YTD perf: 54.53%, Quant rating: 4.98

  2. Oceaneering International (OII), YTD perf: 50.56%, Quant rating: 3.20

  3. Oil States International (OIS), YTD perf: 47.71%, Quant rating: 4.21

  4. Tidewater (TDW), YTD perf: 46.21%, Quant rating: 3.03

  5. Liberty Energy (LBRT), YTD perf: 45.67%, Quant rating: 3.36

  6. Helix Energy Solutions Group (HLX), YTD perf: 45.14%, Quant rating: 3.09

  7. ProFrac Holding (ACDC), YTD perf: 44.47%, Quant rating: 2.31

  8. Forum Energy Technologies (FET), YTD perf: 43.52%, Quant rating: 4.86

  9. TechnipFMC (FTI), YTD perf: 38.60%, Quant rating: 3.31

  10. Baker Hughes (BKR), YTD perf: 37.37%, Quant rating: 3.22

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), and (IXC)

data.title: Consumer sentiment lifts less than anticipated in February
data.description:
Consumer Sentiment Index: 56.6 vs. 57.3 in preliminary reading and 56.4 in January, according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers' final February data.

One-year inflation expectations: +3.4% vs. +3.5% in preliminary and +4.0% prior.

Five-year implied inflation: +3.3% vs. +3.4% preliminary and +3.3% in January.

Current economic conditions: 56.6 vs. 58.3 preliminary and 55.4 prior.

Consumer expectations: 56.6 vs. 56.6 in preliminary and 57.0 prior.

Developing... check back for updates

data.title: Copper fundamentals are ‘extremely good’ – Anglo American CEO
data.description:
Duncan Wanblad, CEO of Anglo American (AAUKF), says the fundamentals for copper (HG1:COM) look “extremely good” as the company positions itself to capitalize on growing demand driven by artificial intelligence, digitalization, and electric vehicles.

In an interview with CNBC, Wanblad expressed confidence in the company’s strategic direction and its heavy focus on the critical metal.

The mining giant has undergone significant portfolio reshaping to emphasize copper-focused growth.

“We are very much a copper-focused growth option for the market,” Wanblad said, noting that the vast majority of the company’s organic growth opportunities are copper-based following the combination of operations.

The optimistic outlook for copper is underpinned by several major demand drivers. AI transformation, digitalization, and the continued expansion of electric vehicles all require substantial amounts of copper, positioning the metal as central to Anglo American’s long-term strategy.

Wanblad emphasized that operational excellence and asset quality matter more than sheer size in navigating the cyclical nature of commodity markets.

“I’m not a really big believer in scale for scale sake,” he said, stressing that assets must be robust enough to survive both the peaks and troughs of market cycles.

The company also addressed a write-down of its De Beers diamond business, attributing the impairment to challenging market dynamics, including a 50% tariff on Indian products exported to the U.S.

However, Wanblad clarified that the impairment does not affect the pace or nature of the ongoing sale process, as all potential buyers are strategic players who understand the cyclical nature of the diamond industry.

Despite the complex challenges across its portfolio, Wanblad expressed confidence in the company’s progress. He noted that Anglo American (AAUKF) has made significant strides in operational excellence, portfolio reshaping, and positioning for growth, crediting the “great people” executing the strategy across all fronts.

Copper and Copper Mining ETFs: (CPER), (COPX), and (JJCTF).

data.title: Tariffs create ‘a lot more economic pain than potential long-term benefits’ – analyst
data.description:
Tony Roth, chief investment officer at Wilmington Trust Investment Advisors, said tariffs imposed by the Trump administration are “creating a lot more economic pain than potential for long-term benefit,” arguing that the trade policy has failed to reduce the trade deficit or trigger the reshoring of American industry that officials promised.

The only upside, Roth noted, is the negotiating leverage tariffs provide the president, though he emphasized that potential trade deals have yet to be finalized.

In an interview with CNBC, Roth said the administration is quietly pursuing a weaker dollar (DXY) despite public statements to the contrary, a move that benefits large multinational exporters.

He predicted the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates three times this year—more than consensus expectations—as a disinflationary trend emerges alongside concerning weakness in the labor market.

The economic outlook Roth painted was one of significant cooling. He noted that GDP growth declined from approximately 4.5% in the third quarter to an expected 2.5% in the fourth quarter, warning that growth could slow to just 1% in the first half of 2026.

“We have a real problem in the labor market, and we have a real problem with the lower-end consumer,” Roth said.

Low-income consumers face an “affordability problem,” burdened not just by current inflation but by the cumulative price increases of recent years, he added. This weakness is already showing up in corporate earnings guidance, with consumer discretionary (XLY) and staples (XLP) companies issuing cautious outlooks even as the broader market reports strong profit growth.

Despite what Roth called a “phenomenal” earnings season with profits growing at 13%, the stock market has been unable to break through the 7,000 level. However, he sees potential tailwinds ahead, particularly from artificial intelligence productivity gains.

“This is the year that that wave of AI that’s been crisscrossing the ocean is really hitting the shore,” he said.

Roth suggested the administration should consider using a potential Supreme Court case as an “off-ramp” to roll back tariffs, which he characterized as “a tax on that consumer, which is struggling so badly.”

Removing these trade barriers, he argued, could provide meaningful relief to the economy and help spark a reacceleration of growth toward the end of the year.
